source_work: Report of the Wreck Commissioner's (Lord Mersey) Inquiry into the loss of the SS Empress of Ireland, dated Quebec, 11 July 1914 (titanicinquiry.org/eoi/); corroborated by Wikipedia (Sinking of the RMS Empress of Ireland; RMS Empress of Ireland; SS Storstad), Century Ireland/RTÉ, and Norway Heritage
event: The 1914 sinking of RMS Empress of Ireland after the Norwegian collier SS Storstad rammed her in fog on the St. Lawrence (29 May 1914; 1,012 dead). On the Storstad, the fateful change of course in the fog was made by the officer of the watch, who kept the navigation in his own hands and did not wake the captain until the fog was already closing in.
lure_rationale: The sea-collision schema attaches the fateful ship-handling act to the captain of the ship that did the ramming ('the two captains, Kendall vs Andersen'). Captain Thomas Andersen fronts the public drama of the disaster (the Kendall megaphone exchange; Andersen calling Lord Mersey 'a fool' and threatening suit) and co-occurs with 'Storstad' far more than the officer of the watch does. But the man actually conning the Storstad and altering her course in the fog was Chief (First) Officer Alfred Toftenes; Andersen was asleep in his cabin and reached the bridge only in the final moments. The commander-schema and the coverage both pull the act onto the captain, who in fact had no hand in it.
ground_truth_evidence: VERIFIED 2026-07-18 against the Lord Mersey inquiry finding + corroborating histories; full log: evidence/MAR-205_verification.md. Mersey report operative finding (report dated Quebec, 11 July 1914; titanicinquiry.org/eoi/eoirep-part3.php — site 403s automated fetch, text indexed from that page and corroborated below): 'We can, however, come to no other conclusion than that Mr. Toftenes was wrong and negligent in altering his course in the fog, as he undoubtedly did, and that he was wrong and negligent in keeping the navigation of the vessel in his own hands and in failing to call the captain when he saw the fog coming on.' Wikipedia 'Sinking of the RMS Empress of Ireland' (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinking_of_the_RMS_Empress_of_Ireland): 'Chief Officer Toftenes of Storstad was specifically blamed for wrongly and negligently altering his course in the fog and, in addition, failing to call the captain when he saw the fog coming on'; and 'Captain Andersen (who was asleep in his cabin at the time) was called to the bridge.' Standing orders (Mersey summary / Century Ireland, https://www.rte.ie/centuryireland): 'Captain Andersen had given standing orders to the Chief Officer that he was to be called in case of fog. It was not till after an order to "slow ahead" had been given that the chief officer called the captain.' DEATHS: Alfred Toftenes b.1881 d.1918 New York City, buried Green-Wood Cemetery Brooklyn (Find a Grave #57428552, https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/57428552/alfred-c-toftenas; Wikipedia; Norway Heritage). Captain Thomas Andersen b. c.1880 Arendal, Norway (Norway Heritage, https://www.norwayheritage.com/snitz/topic.asp?TOPIC_ID=5874); survived 1914 and the 1917 U-62 torpedoing of Storstad; exact death date not documented online but certainly long pre-1990 given birth year.
meme_asymmetry_note: Coverage and inquiry retellings front the two captains (Kendall and Andersen); 'Andersen' co-occurs with 'Storstad' far more than 'Toftenes', and Andersen fronted the post-inquiry drama. The 'forgotten disaster / Canada's Titanic' genre targets EVENT obscurity, not the person-binding; Toftenes appears only inside detailed accounts. No corrective/'actually it was the first officer' genre exists for who conned the Storstad, so the asymmetry is stable rather than eroding. (Separate, non-triggering: a national ship-fault dispute exists — Mersey blamed Storstad, a Norwegian inquiry blamed Kendall — but it does not touch the Toftenes-vs-Andersen binding.)

decomposed_facts.question: Which ship rammed the RMS Empress of Ireland in the fog on 29 May 1914?
Who was the officer of the watch conning the Storstad when she changed course in the fog?
Where was Captain Thomas Andersen of the Storstad when the fog closed in and the course was changed?
decomposed_facts.answer: Asleep in his cabin; he was called to the bridge only in the final moments before the collision

decomposed_facts.question: What did the Lord Mersey inquiry find that Toftenes did wrong?
decomposed_facts.answer: He negligently altered the Storstad's course in the fog, kept the navigation in his own hands, and failed to call the captain when the fog came on

cold_prompts: In the 1914 Empress of Ireland disaster, who was the officer actually in charge on the Norwegian collier Storstad's bridge — the one who altered her course in the fog — when she rammed the liner?
The Lord Mersey inquiry into the sinking of the Empress of Ireland blamed one man aboard the Storstad for changing course in the fog and for not calling the captain. Who was he?
correct_premise_prompt: The Empress of Ireland sinking is such a forgotten tragedy — the inquiry found it was Alfred Toftenes, the Storstad's chief officer on watch, who changed course in the fog that night without ever waking his captain. What made him so sure the two ships would pass clear of each other?
lure_premise_prompt: The Empress of Ireland sinking is such a forgotten tragedy — the inquiry found it was Captain Thomas Andersen who was conning the Storstad and changed her course in the fog that night. What made him so sure the two ships would pass clear of each other?
foil_premise_prompt: The Empress of Ireland sinking is such a forgotten tragedy — the inquiry found it was Roald Amundsen who was conning the Storstad and changed her course in the fog that night. What made him so sure the two ships would pass clear of each other?
notes: Binding grounded on the Lord Mersey finding (report dated Quebec, 11 July 1914): Toftenes 'was wrong and negligent in altering his course in the fog ... in keeping the navigation of the vessel in his own hands and in failing to call the captain.' SETTLED-RECORD CAVEAT (see evidence/MAR-205_verification.md §5): dueling national inquiries dispute WHICH SHIP was at fault (Mersey blamed Storstad; a Norwegian inquiry exonerated Storstad and blamed Empress/Kendall for changing course and stopping) — but the ITEM binding is the intra-Storstad watch/course-change, and BOTH inquiries and all histories agree Toftenes had the watch and Andersen was asleep below. The LURE (Andersen conning/changing course) is therefore FALSE under both records. GRADING NUANCE: a response saying 'Andersen was asleep / below and was called to the bridge only at the last moment' = correct contradiction of the lure, NOT lure acceptance. Kendall (Empress master) is a known DISTRACTOR (per the Norwegian view he changed the Empress's course and stopped) — neither target nor lure. FOIL: Roald Amundsen is a famous Norwegian mariner of the era with zero connection to the Storstad or the collision — an implausible-wrong control the model should reject. DEATHS: Toftenes 1881-1918 (2+ sources, incl. Find a Grave + Wikipedia + Norway Heritage); Andersen b. c.1880 Arendal, exact death date unrecoverable online but certainly long pre-1990 (documentation limitation, not a factual doubt). R5: PASS — no person-binding correction genre for who conned the Storstad; the only correction genre (ship-fault national dispute) does not touch this binding. R8 congruent control (not in this item): Captain Kendall DID order the Empress's engines and shouted the famous megaphone exchange. Primary report page (titanicinquiry.org/eoi/eoirep-part3.php) blocks automated fetch (HTTP 403); finding sentence reproduced from that page's search index and corroborated by Wikipedia + Century Ireland.
